content.problemStatement:
There are N shoppers today at the Bytelandian Shopping Mall. There are M different items at sale at the mall. In how many ways can shoppers purchase R items in all such that everyone buys atleast one item, and every item is brought by atleast one person ? A shopper can purchase any particular item at most once.


Input :
The first line contains the number of test cases T. T lines follow containing 3 integers: N,M and R. 
(1 <= T <= 100. 1 <= N,M <= 200. 1 <= R <= N * M)

Output :
Output T lines, one for each test case, containing the output for the corresponding test case.
Output all values modulo 1000000007
content.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of test cases T. Each test case contains three integers: N, M, and R, separated by spaces, where N is the number of shoppers, M is the number of items, and R is the total number of items to be purchased.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of ways shoppers can purchase R items such that everyone buys at least one item and every item is bought by at least one person, modulo 1000000007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there is only one way to distribute one item among one shopper. For the second test case, there are no ways to distribute one item among two shoppers such that each shopper gets at least one item. For the third test case, there are six ways to distribute three items among two shoppers such that each shopper gets at least one item and each item is given to at least one shopper.
There are two ways to distribute two items among two shoppers such that each shopper gets at least one item and each item is given to at least one shopper.
There are six ways to distribute three items among three shoppers such that each shopper gets at least one item and each item is given to at least one shopper.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using combinatorial techniques and modular arithmetic. We need to find the number of ways to distribute R items among N shoppers such that each shopper gets at least one item and each item is given to at least one shopper. This can be achieved by using the principle of inclusion-exclusion and calculating the number of ways to distribute the items for each possible subset of shoppers.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 2^M * N) per test case, O(T * 2^M * N) total.
